q: What is confluence triple X and how is it different from earlier confluence videos?
a: Triple X means naked — pure. You're stripping away the clutter and relying on pure price action and deep structural understanding. By the time you get to this level, you shouldn't need fibs plastered all over your chart 24/7 to understand what you're seeing. It's less disorder, more clarity. But you can't skip the foundation — you have to go through 1.0, 2.0, 2.5, 2.9, 3.0 first.

q: What are the main tools you use most often when reading confluence on a chart?
a: Exhaustion areas, trendlines, counter-trendlines, and fibs — but fibs at a minimum at the advanced level. You're looking at candle analysis, understanding where R and R sits, and above all, patience. These are the things that show up over and over again across every setup. You don't need much on the chart if you understand what you're actually looking for.

q: How do you use fibs in a confluence setup — what's point A and what's point B?
a: Point A is the high, point B is the low — or vice versa depending on direction. Bearish to bearish, bullish to bullish, always match the direction. From there your retracement levels tell you where to expect structure. You're not just throwing fibs everywhere — you find the candles that actually established the support or resistance level, and that's your anchor. The numbers tell the story, but the structure confirms it.

q: What does 23.6% on the fib tell you about the continuation of a move?
a: When price sticks around 23.6%, that's continuation. It means the market doesn't have enough volume to even pull back to 38.2%, so it's going to keep moving in the original direction. Say it with me — 23.6% means continuation. That said, that area can also turn into future resistance once price makes a new low and comes back to retest it. Pay attention to those levels because they'll show up again.

q: Why is the 38.2% fib level so important when looking for a first higher low entry?
a: The 38.2% is your first valid higher low point opportunity. When price closes at 38.2%, it qualifies as a higher low — that's not opinion, that's structure. On a countertrend, the minimum I'm looking for is at least 38.2% to confirm that area before I'm looking for buys. Anything inside 23.6% is too shallow — wait. 38.2% with the MAs in alignment is straight confluence.

q: What role do exhaustion areas play in your confluence system?
a: Exhaustion areas are zones where the market is struggling — it wants to push through but keeps getting rejected with long wicks. When you see multiple exhaustion wicks in the same area across M15, M30, and H1, that's not noise, that's a real level. If you see exhaustion on one timeframe but a higher timeframe clears it out, that exhaustion is just noise. Exhaustion showing on multiple timeframes means that area is real.

q: How do you confirm that a trendline break is valid before entering a trade?
a: You need two closed candles beyond the trendline — not fluctuating volume, closed volume. One candle can fake you out. Two candles closed beyond the level tells you that area is genuinely broken. From there you're looking for a retest of that broken trendline before entering. You also want to see that break confirmed on your M15 or M30 entry timeframe, not just assumed from a higher frame.

q: What is a bearish variation and why do you need one before you place a fib?
a: A bearish variation is bullish, bearish — that sequence on the candles at a resistance level. It's what establishes the resistance. I'm not placing a fib until I see that confirmation. If you have bullish, doji — that's not it yet. Wait. Next candle. Once you get that bullish, bearish close, that's your point A. The resistance is now established and your fib is valid.

q: What does the stack look like when everything is aligned for a high-probability entry?
a: The stack is when all your confluences are hitting at the same time — structure, fib level, exhaustion, MAs in position, trendline, and lower timeframe confirmation. When I had that setup at 38.2% on M30 with the MAs acting as support, exhaustion forming, and a bullish variation showing — that's the stack. You shouldn't be afraid to enter when the stack is there. You should be more aggressive when you have razonamientos for everything.

q: How do moving averages factor into your confluence entries?
a: On the lower timeframes — M30 especially — price will often pull back to try to hit the MAs. Those MA bounces can be golden. If the MA is below the market the entire time in an uptrend, that's a dynamic level of support. When price comes down and hits the MA and shows exhaustion or a bullish variation, that's where you dig to the lower timeframe and look for entries. MAs also help you read the slope — a down slope on a higher timeframe means you're in a downtrend overall.

q: How do you build a bias across multiple timeframes before going to a lower timeframe for entries?
a: Every week I write down the bias for monthly, weekly, daily, and H4 on a whiteboard. If monthly is bearish, weekly is bearish, daily is bearish, and H4 is bearish — your job is to find sells, not buys. You can still catch countertrend moves but you can't hold them long. When multiple timeframes agree, that's where the big moves come from. Knowing your bias keeps you from holding the wrong direction at the wrong time.

q: When the overall bias is bearish, how long can you hold a countertrend buy?
a: Not long. If the monthly and weekly are bearish and I see a buy opportunity on M30 or H1, that's a countertrend — short-term only. The market is still overall bearish and it could just be a retracement preparing another push down. I take the move, grab the pips, but I'm not trying to ride it to the sky. My minimum target on a countertrend is at least 38.2% on the fib before I even consider it a valid higher low.

q: What entry timeframe do you use when price is at a 23.6% retracement?
a: At 23.6%, go to M5 for entries. The move is shallow, there's not much data on the countertrend, and you need the detail. On deeper retracements like 38.2%, 50%, or 61.8%, you can go M15 or M30 because you've got more candle data to work with — thicker candles mean more confirmation. At 23.6%, M5 gives you enough to analyze without overcomplicating it.

q: How do you use counter-trendlines to identify when a countertrend is ending?
a: Counter-trendlines tell you when the countertrend is actually done. During a downtrend, price will create countertrend moves — I draw those lines point A to point B and watch for the break. Once that counter-trendline breaks, that's your signal that the main trend is resuming. It's not just about the break — look for a retest of that broken line and then enter on the continuation. These lines are critical for knowing when the counter move is finished.

q: What does it mean when exhaustion shows up on M5, M15, and M30 at the same time?
a: That area is real and valid — no debate. When exhaustion is visible on multiple timeframes simultaneously, that's not noise, that's a strong level. If you only see exhaustion on one timeframe and a higher timeframe clears it, ignore it. But when it's showing up on M5, M15, and M30 all at once, that zone is a definitive area of interest and you can feel confident building your case around it.

q: What is the difference between a valid support level and noise on a higher timeframe?
a: Zoom out. If you see consolidation on a lower timeframe that looks like support, zoom out to monthly or weekly. If those longer timeframes just show wicks with no real structure around that area, what you were seeing was noise. A valid support level needs to show up on multiple timeframes. If it doesn't show on the higher frame, it didn't establish anything — it's just movement inside a larger candle.

q: How do you set your stop loss based on confluence analysis?
a: Stop loss goes below the lowest wick in the exhaustion zone — not just below the candle body, below the wick. On M15 entries I'll often use M30 stop placement because it gives the trade room to breathe without being too wide. On US 30, I use 100 points as a standard because that market will stop you out if you're too tight. Give enough space for the fluctuation zone, but never so much that a losing trade becomes catastrophic.

q: What does smooth price action look like, and why does it matter for building confluence?
a: When it's smooth it's like butter — clear higher lows, MAs below the market acting as support, clean upward moves with nice retracements to 38.2%. No choppy back-and-forth, no fighting every level. When price is acting like butter on H1, that's when you know the setup is clean enough to hold. The cleaner the structure, the more confluence you have to work with because every pullback gives you a valid entry opportunity.

q: Why do you need to modify your fib after a new high or low is made?
a: Once a new high point is made, the old fib is done — it's no longer measuring the correct move. Clear it off and wait for a new resistance to form with a bearish variation, then place point A to point B fresh. If you leave the old fib on the chart, you'll be targeting the wrong levels and misreading the structure. Charts cluttered with old fibs will have you chasing a direction that's not there anymore.

q: How does the moving average slope confirm the overall market direction in your analysis?
a: I'll take a random line and mimic the slope of the MA to see what direction it's actually pointing — down, sideways, or up. If the MA on the daily is sloping down consistently, the majority of price action is bearish in that area. That slope on higher timeframes tells you what's expected even on lower timeframe moves. The more volatile the market, the steeper the MA slope will become as it confirms direction.

q: Walk me through your top-down process for US 30 before entering a trade.
a: I start monthly — is it bullish or bearish based on the candle? Move to weekly, then daily, then H4. I write the bias for each timeframe on a whiteboard. Once I know my biases, I go to H4 for overall structure and fib placement, H1 for more detail on the flow, then drop to M30 and M15 for entries. On US 30, always be ready by 9:30 AM Eastern — that's when volatility kicks in hard and your technicals need to be done before that.

q: What makes a double bottom valid in a confluence setup?
a: A double bottom confirms support and is valid in an uptrend. It's telling you that area is a strong level being respected twice, and the buyers are still showing up. A double top is only valid in a downtrend — don't flip them. When you see a double bottom forming at or near your fib level with the MAs below the market, that's adding another layer of confluence to an already solid setup.

q: How do you scale into multiple entries in the same direction using confluence?
a: Every entry you add needs to meet the same standard as the first one — don't get lazy with the second or third entry just because the trade is already working. Treat all entries equally. Your first trade is your heaviest because it has the most room for the full move. As you scale in, you can tighten stop losses on earlier entries since you've confirmed direction. All entries flow to the same TP based on your main fib projection.

q: Why shouldn't you enter in the middle of an exhaustion area when looking for a continuation sell?
a: If price is already deep in an exhaustion zone with multiple wicks firing in multiple timeframes, you're arriving late and it's not attractive. You don't want seller exhaustion or buyer exhaustion in your entry candle because you'll immediately see unnecessary drawdown. Wait for the exhaustion to clear, look for a break of the counter-trendline, and then find the retest. Entering inside chaos means you're setting yourself up to fail even if the overall direction is right.

q: When should you exit a trade early versus holding toward the full projection target?
a: When you start seeing heavy exhaustion on the H4 frame, multiple wicks forming at a level, and a break of a trendline — that's your sign to get out or take partial. If the candle is closing in a way that looks suspect across M15, M30, and H1 all at the same time, I'm getting out. I'd rather step away with a clean head and same bias than hold through something that tells me momentum is shifting. Re-entry is always possible.

q: What do you look for on a lower timeframe to confirm a higher timeframe confluence setup is ready for entry?
a: I want a break of a counter-trendline on the lower timeframe, a retest of that broken level, and then a bullish or bearish variation — two closed candles showing the variation, not just one. The entry candle should not have exhaustion wicks on the wrong side. When I drop to M5 on a tight area, I'm looking for clarity — a clean candle close that tells me momentum is there and not just fluctuating. Structure on top plus a clean candle below equals the entry.

q: What does 'tick by tick' actually mean in your trading approach?
a: It means you gotta be able to analyze each and every single move, each and every single time that the market makes a brand new candle. You're not just setting it and forgetting it — you're reading what that candle means, how it's forming, what it stands for when it closes. Once you stick with a certain timeframe and understand what the overall analysis is doing on a higher timeframe, you can be on the lower timeframe with clarity and not fall victim to the noise.

q: How much time should I actually be spending on each timeframe?
a: H four is about 30% of your time — that's your main analysis timeframe. M five is 60% of your time, that's where you're actively watching entries and exits tick by tick. M30 and H one are only about 10% of the time combined — very quick, very subtle checks. Don't get it twisted and start living on the H one when your main analysis belongs on the H four.

q: Why do you always say to wait for candle closes? Can't I just enter when I see the setup forming?
a: Waiting for candle closes is one of the biggest determining factors of what you're gonna do next, and when it's not done, everything is thrown off. Say it's 7:43 and you jump in a five minute trade — by the time that candle closes it's a full wick and now you're negative for no reason. You could've waited to see how that candle was gonna close to then give you better confirmation to enter or stay out. Live by the candle close.

q: What is the WSA order of operations when you're analyzing a chart?
a: Structure is always first — always. Then your MAs, then your candlestick analysis, then everything else you plan on analyzing. Regardless of what the MAs are doing, always ask yourself what is structure doing first so you can apply everything else to that. That order is gonna stop the impulsiveness that hits every now and again.

q: How do you use the EMAs to decide your directional bias?
a: Simple — if the market is above the MAs, focus on buys because that's where the majority of the momentum is. If the market is under the MAs, focus on sells. The angle of the MAs matters too. If the MAs are pointing down and you're trying to buy, you're gonna get hit every single time. Stick with the f*****g plan and flow with the MAs.

q: What does it mean when the H4 is showing a doji while I'm trying to trade M5?
a: A doji just means pause — the market is on pause for a little bit. It could stay that way for a while and that's perfectly fine. As long as the lower timeframes are maintaining the structure and direction you're hoping for, a doji on H four doesn't change your bias. Never try to rush that concept. Let it pause, just make sure the support holds on the lower timeframe.

q: How do I know if a bearish candle on H1 should actually change my bullish bias?
a: Correlate it with what the M five is showing you. If the M five doesn't support the bearish move — meaning it's still above the MAs and not breaking support — then that H one bearish candle is not intimidating enough to shift your mind. If that H one bearish candle had a break of support on M five and the price was under the MAs, then maybe I'd believe it. Don't let one candle on a higher timeframe override what multiple timeframes are actually telling you.

q: What's the purpose of always asking yourself 'what time is it?' during analysis?
a: It keeps you from making premature decisions based on incomplete candles. If you're on H one and that candle has nine minutes left, that candle still has an opportunity to change character. On M five I can look at what's happening inside that H one candle to see what's likely going on. Knowing how many minutes are left in each candle is tick by tick awareness — it determines your next move completely.

q: How do you use fibs to find your entry on a setup you've already identified?
a: You draw point A to point B — from the top of the move to the bottom, or bottom to top depending on direction. The 38.2% is your first higher low point entry for a bullish structure, and that is your key area. The 61.8% is another major level to watch, and I use -27% as my projection area for the TP. If price retraces to only 23.6% that's too shallow — I'm not counting it as a true higher low in most cases.

q: What is confluence to you — how do you define it?
a: Confluence is just when things align with each other, that's literally all it is. If you have two confirmations that align with each other in the same area, that's confluence. A 61.8% fib lining up with a previous resistance level that's now support — that's confluence. The more things stack on the same zone, the more confidence you have to pull the trigger on that trade.

q: How do you handle a setup when the higher timeframe MA is acting as resistance against your trade direction?
a: You have to be more cautious and factor it into your TP expectations. If you're bullish on M five but you're approaching a higher timeframe MA on H four, you might experience struggle or consolidation trying to break it. I'll still aim bullish if the lower timeframes support it, but I'm realistic that the market might hit resistance there. Zoom out, see where those MAs are sitting, and adjust your TP accordingly.

q: When a market is consolidating, what's the first thing you ask yourself?
a: Always ask yourself why it's consolidating — a lot of times it's sitting right on a higher timeframe MA and you didn't even realize it. You go back and see it was right there the whole time, hidden in plain sight. That's why you gotta stay aware of what's happening on each and every timeframe. Once you understand it's sitting on a higher timeframe resistance, you're not confused anymore — you're just waiting for the break.

q: What's the difference between a break of structure and a break of resistance in your analysis?
a: A break of structure is when actual structure points are being broken — your higher lows or lower highs depending on trend. A break of resistance is simpler — it just means price pushed through a level that was holding it back. Sometimes I'm not even looking for a break of structure, just a break of resistance to confirm buyers or sellers are taking control. You have to know which one you're actually looking for in that specific situation.

q: How do you scale into a trade using M1 and M5 together?
a: When I already know where the market is going based on higher timeframe analysis, I go down to M five and even M one to find the best entry possible. I'm watching every small detail — each candle, each little support level, each exhaustion area — to find multiple entry points as the trend continues. One entry at the breakout, another at the retrace, another as it continues pushing. That way you're compounding within the same trend instead of just taking one shot.

q: How do you manage being in drawdown without closing out prematurely?
a: As long as your risk is at a key point that is out of the fluctuation zone, you're good — f**k the drawdown. The fluctuation that happens within the trade is what you signed up for when you entered. You put a signature saying you're open to risking this, and that fluctuation is what's going to give you the reward you're looking for. Don't close out just because the candle is red — if the structure hasn't broken and your risk level is intact, you hold.

q: When should you actually look for a counter-trend trade?
a: When you see major exhaustion on the higher timeframe — long wicks, seller exhaustion candles, or buyer exhaustion depending on direction — that gives you enough reason to take a counter-trend move. I'm looking at the daily timeframe first for that confirmation before taking anything counter to the main trend. But you have to understand it's a counter-trend, keep it short, and get back with the main trend once it confirms the reversal.

q: Why does candle age matter when you're reading weekly or monthly timeframes?
a: Candles at the beginning of a week or month aren't fully valid yet because they have a long life to go. I look at monthly candles mostly at the end of the month because that's when they're most valid — the candle is about to close and it's telling you a complete story. A candle that closes beyond a previous level means something totally different than one that's still forming with three weeks left. Always have candle life in mind when you're analyzing higher timeframes.

q: How do you know when price approaching a zone aggressively changes the outcome?
a: If price comes into a zone very aggressively with strong closed candles, it has a high chance of breaking through it. If price approaches cautiously — wicking into the area, small indecisive candles — it probably doesn't have enough momentum to break and will reject. Watch how that first approach happens, because it tells you everything about whether buyers or sellers have enough power to follow through.

q: You talked about 42 consecutive wins. What were you doing differently in that period?
a: I was staying in my system — H four for the main analysis, M five for the active trading the majority of the time, and not skipping steps. The moment I started feeling myself and stopped doing the small things — not checking that certain timeframe for updates, not going down to my entry timeframe like I normally would — the losses started. It could be something so small, but it's so vital. The rhythm of how you're trading just flows smoothly when you're in the right mindset.

q: What does a higher low point forming inside a consolidation zone actually signal?
a: When you start seeing higher low points form inside a consolidation, that's the indication the market is getting stronger for buys at that point in time. Each higher low is buyers showing up at slightly higher prices — they're not waiting as long anymore. That's the signal you're looking for before a breakout. Once that consolidation breaks, those higher low points already told you exactly what was coming.

q: Why do you use -27% as your projection target instead of standard extension levels?
a: I use -27% as my projection area because there's almost always a point in time where the market will try to make a high or a low, and those support and resistance areas are almost always aligning with that -27% level. It's cleaner for me and it keeps me disciplined on where I'm aiming. I was using expansions more back in 2.0 and 2.5, but -27% gives me a consistent target to hold trades to without second-guessing.

q: What does timeframe correlation actually mean in practical terms?
a: Timeframe correlation means understanding what's happening at each level of the chart and how the lower timeframe activity is reflecting the higher timeframe story. Once you understand why price did what it did on M five, you can see how it connects to what H four is building. It's not just switching between charts — you're understanding why price acted that way and using that to profit from the lower timeframe details. Time correlation is huge.

q: How do you avoid falling victim to short-term noise when you're in a trending market?
a: Float the trend. If you're on an overall uptrend on H four, stay on M five and wait until the market actually gives you a bullish opportunity in that direction. Don't try to catch five-pip or two-pip short-term moves going against the trend. Short-term moves are noise. The majority of the big moves are gonna be with the trend, so you have to be patient enough to wait for those and not get shaken out by every little pullback.

q: When a setup has a 61.8% retrace with the EMAs crossing at the same zone, how do you read that?
a: That's two confirmations aligning — that's confluence right there. A 61.8% retrace is already a key fib level showing a meaningful pullback, and when you add an EMA cross at the same area showing buyers or sellers taking over, you've got multiple things telling you the same story. That's the kind of stack that gives me confidence to pull the trigger, especially if structure is also supporting it on that timeframe.

q: What should I do when I see a gap form on the M5 chart?
a: Plot it off immediately — that gap has to fill. But never try to get on a gap fill the second it forms. You want to see a break of support in that area first, and then look for an entry based on how price confirms the fill. A lot of times these gaps fill coming close to New York session, so you might be sitting there at 6am or 7am just watching and waiting. Mark it, be patient, let the setup develop.

q: What's the biggest mistake you see traders making when they have back to back losses?
a: Withdrawing their entire account every time they take a loss. You have win, lose, win, lose — that's literally all trading is. If you withdraw the majority of your account after a loss, now you're trading a smaller lot size mentally and you're not gonna be there anymore. If you want to withdraw, withdraw to a point where you're still able to do the same lot size you were doing prior. Don't let one loss shift your entire analysis flow and sizing.

q: What is Confluence Trading and why did you name your system that?
a: Confluence Trading is trading based on multiple confirmations within a market — when things align, they give you a powerful reason as to why you're entering the market at a certain moment. It's not just one thing telling you to get in. It's support and resistance, structure, trendlines, Fibonacci, and patterns all lining up at the same time. The more confluences you have, the more confidence you have in your order.

q: What is the foundation of your entire trading style?
a: The base of my trading style is support and resistance — that's almost everything the market is built on. Every pattern, every strategy, everything else is based on support and resistance. When you're reading support and resistance, you're reading the raw base of the market compared to using a bunch of indicator-based strategies. When you can actually read the general base of the market, you can literally catch almost every move the market has to make.

q: What are the main tools you use in your trading system?
a: Nothing crazy — I keep it simple. I use support and resistance zones, trendlines, Fibonacci for retracements and projections, market structure, and candlestick patterns. All of it ties back to support and resistance as the foundation. The indicators I use are minimal because the more I learn, the less I put on my charts.

q: How do you organize your support and resistance levels by timeframe?
a: My daily levels are red horizontal lines on the line chart. My H4 levels are green — not bright green, not super dark, but that middle green. H1 levels are orange. And my random levels, the ones I find when I'm zooming in looking for entries, those are blue. When you color-code your levels by timeframe, you always know exactly what you're looking at when you open a chart.

q: Why do you call support and resistance levels 'indecision levels'?
a: Because that's exactly what they are — the market is indecisive at those prices. Every time price comes back to that level, the market slows down, tries to figure out where it wants to go, and either rejects or breaks. When a level has acted as both support AND resistance over time, that's golden — the market keeps coming back to that price and can't make up its mind. That indecision is your opportunity.

q: How do you mark support and resistance levels on the daily chart?
a: I go to the line chart — not the candlestick chart — for my dailies. I'm looking for areas where price has been indecisive multiple times. If a zone acted as resistance, then support, then resistance again, that's golden. I place the line at the median of the entire area so it touches as many points as possible, not at the lowest wick or the highest. Common sense is your guide — don't overcomplicate it.

q: How many H4 support and resistance levels do you look for between each daily zone?
a: Two max three H4 indecision levels between each daily zone. That's it. I hate trying to pack support levels into my zones — if I can find two, great. If I can only find one quality level, I'm not forcing a second. Between every two daily zones there are areas, and I'm only marking the strongest H4 levels inside those areas.

q: What's the difference between a support level being tested versus being broken?
a: These are two completely different things and most people confuse them. When the market tests a level, it reaches that price or closes at it, and then the next candle goes in the opposite direction — it's being respected. When the market breaks a level, it forms through it AND the next candle continues in that same direction. If you're on a lower timeframe like 15 or 30 minutes, you'll see what looks like a break when it's actually just a test. That's the noise trapping people.

q: Why do you prefer higher timeframes for reading market structure?
a: Higher timeframes show you less noise and more clarity. When you're on a 15 or 30-minute chart, you're seeing too much movement, too many unnecessary wicks — stuff that can fake you out completely. When I go to H4, the patterns are thick. It takes a lot of market movement to actually create those patterns, which means every pattern on H4 carries way more weight than the same pattern on M15.

q: What is market structure and why do you say it's the first thing to look at?
a: Structure first, always. Structure is the sequence of highs and lows the market is making — in an uptrend it's higher highs and higher lows, in a downtrend it's lower highs and lower lows. If you don't know the structure, you can't know the trend, and if you don't know the trend, you'll get faked out by every counter-move the market makes. People who know structure don't fall for unnecessary wicks and spikes because they know the general trend and what a broken trend looks like.

q: How do you know when market structure has actually broken?
a: Structure breaks when the market takes out a previous significant level and creates a new candle confirming it went through. In a downtrend, structure breaks when the market takes out a lower high — once that happens, you're no longer looking for sells long-term. It's literally finished. The same way in a trend — if the market never breaks a previous higher low, the uptrend is still valid no matter how scary the counter-move looks on a lower timeframe.

q: How do you draw Fibonacci from Point A to Point B?
a: In an uptrend, your Point A is the lowest point and your Point B is the highest point — you're measuring from low to high. In a downtrend, it's high to low. What you're actually measuring with fibs is the counter-trend move based on the current trend. The market has to stay below the 100% level to remain valid in the structure. I like to base Point A on the most important previous low, not just any swing.

q: What is the 38.2% Fibonacci level and why does it matter for entries?
a: The 38.2 is your first significant retracement level. In a strong uptrend with enough sellers entering to create a pullback, the market will come back to 38.2 and that's where I'm looking for my entry setup. If the trend is super strong and there aren't enough opposing market participants, the market might only pull back to 23.6 before pushing again. But if there are enough sellers to create a real retracement, 38.2 is your first key area.

q: What are Fibonacci expansions and how are they different from retracements?
a: Retracements measure how far a counter-trend move goes — that's your entry zone. Expansions are a three-step process that project where the market is going after that retracement. Expansions are basically the cousin of retracements, they're in the same Fibonacci family. You use your Point A, Point B, and then where the market found support during the retracement — that's your Point C — to get your projection target.

q: How do Fibonacci expansion projections work with different retracement levels?
a: Where the market finds structure during the retracement tells you exactly where your expansion target is. If the market retraces to 38.2, your projection is 224% to 261.8%. If it finds structure at 50%, project to 200%. At 61.8%, project to 161.8%. At 78.6%, project to 127%. At 88.6%, you're looking at 113%. The deeper the retracement, the shorter the expansion — it's a direct relationship.

q: What candlestick patterns do you look for when entering a trade?
a: In an uptrend, I'm only looking for bullish engulfing patterns — that's the only valid entry pattern because it shows buyers are in control. A bearish engulfing in an uptrend is invalid to me because sellers aren't supposed to have control in a bullish trend. In a downtrend, I'm only looking for bearish engulfing patterns at lower highs. If I see a doji I cross out both sides and put the patterns from left and right together — that gives me my pattern.

q: Why do you wait for the third candle after a reversal pattern before entering?
a: Because after a reversal pattern forms, the candle that comes right after it often tries to fake people out — it'll retrace slightly in the opposite direction before continuing the move. That's the market trying to shake out weak hands. Once that third candle forms and confirms the direction, that's your entry candle. I always wait for that third candle, always. The market wants to trick you before it moves.

q: What does it mean when a pattern is 'valid' versus 'invalid' based on trend?
a: A pattern is only valid if it aligns with the current trend. A bullish engulfing in an uptrend at a higher low is valid — buyers confirming control. That same bullish pattern at a lower high in a downtrend is invalid because buyers shouldn't have control in a counter-trend move. You can't take every pattern you see. You have to know what trend you're in first, then you know which patterns to pay attention to and which ones to ignore.

q: Why do you focus on just one pair instead of trading multiple pairs?
a: Find a pair and dominate that pair. Don't jump around, don't try to trade two pairs at once — none of that. My favorite pair is AUD/USD because it's commodity-based, moves very slow and steady, but when it moves it moves a lot and shows very clear setups. I make money from that pair almost every single day. Compared to someone jumping from pair to pair trying to find setups — that makes no sense to me.

q: How do you analyze the market top-down starting from the daily chart?
a: You start on the daily line chart and mark all your significant indecision levels across your entire price history — focusing on what's left of where price currently is, because that's what's relevant right now. Then you drop to H4 line chart and find two to three levels between each of your daily zones. Then you go to H1 using candlesticks, not the line chart, for your more detailed levels. Structure, then levels, then execution from there.

q: What do you mean when you say the monthly timeframe gives you 'key levels'?
a: The monthly is the highest timeframe we look at and those levels carry the most weight of anything on your chart. I look for where price has respected strong support and strong resistance at the monthly level and mark that as my key level. Then the 50% between those two monthly levels is also valid because it acts as a midpoint where the market finds indecision. I use those monthly key levels as the overall target area I'm working toward on lower timeframes.

q: Why do you use the line chart instead of candlesticks when marking daily and H4 levels?
a: Because the line chart shows you the median of the price action — it filters out all the wicks and noise so you can see the pure price movement. When I'm finding indecision areas on daily and H4, I want to see where price consistently agreed on a certain level, not where a random wick spiked to. On H1 I switch to candlesticks because I want to see the wicks for finding entries — those weak areas with lots of wicks are where I draw my H1 zones.

q: What are 'weak areas' on the H1 chart and how do you use them?
a: Weak areas are the zones with a lot of wicks on the H1 candlestick chart. I'm not going to the lowest wick or the highest wick — I'm finding the median of the entire area, a zone that touches as many wicks as possible across the board. Those wicks represent where the market was fighting but couldn't fully commit. Once I find that median and draw it, I check if it's been respected going all the way back to the left side — often you'll see it was still being respected months ago.

q: How do counter-trend moves fit into the Confluence Trading system?
a: Counter-trends are normal — if you're in a bullish market for 10 to 13 days, you're going to have counter-trend moves because sellers are trying to enter and balance the market. Those aren't reasons to panic or flip your bias. What I'm doing during counter-trend moves is watching for higher lows to hold and looking for bullish patterns to confirm continuation. If the market never breaks a previous higher low, the uptrend is still valid and I'm still only looking for buys.

q: What's the minimum number of reasons you need before entering a trade?
a: Two to three reasons minimum — always. You need your Fibonacci level, your support or resistance zone, your market structure, and ideally a reversal pattern to confirm. If you don't have multiple reasons to enter the market, then most likely you're entering for the wrong reasons. The more reasons you have, the more confidence you have in the order — and more confidence means you hold the trade without second-guessing every candle.

q: What risk management percentage do you recommend for a new trader with a $1,000 account?
a: I recommend 1% to 5% — that's your scale from conservative to the absolute most I'd go as a new trader. At 5% on a $1,000 account with 1:1000 leverage, you're trading 0.50 lots, which means you're gaining or losing $5 per pip. Even in a worst case scenario where you lose your first three trades at 30 pips each, that's only $450 gone — and when you win your next seven, you're $600 in profit with a $1,600 account. That's duplicatable. That compounds.

q: What happens if a new trader uses 10% risk management instead of 5%?
a: At 10% on a $1,000 account, you're trading a full standard lot, losing or making $10 per pip. If you lose your first three trades at 30 pips each, that's $300 per trade — close to $900 gone total. Your account balance wouldn't even be able to sustain trading a lot anymore. You'd be in a fearful mindset because you just lost $900, and you can't even continue your next seven trades. That's how accounts blow.

q: Why do you say your strategy isn't the basis of your trading success?
a: Most people think that if they're not winning, they need to learn another strategy, join another group, buy a scanner — whatever. That's wrong. It has nothing to do with your trading strategy and everything to do with understanding risk management. I know traders right now posting on social media every day in profit, and those same traders are coming to me asking what I'm doing — because their risk management is broken.

q: How do you handle a trade after you hit your minimum 30 pips?
a: Once I make 30 pips, I manually trail the trade from there. If it goes up another 10 pips, I'll set my stop-loss higher — so now I've gone from 30 pips to 35 pips. If it goes up another 10, I move that to maybe 40 or 45. That $600 profit number I always use is very variable — you could have one trade go up 100 pips and win $500 in a single trade. The 30 pip minimum is your floor, not your ceiling.

q: What's the worst case scenario you should always plan for before entering a trade?
a: If you're winning 70% of your trades, that means you're losing 30%. The worst case scenario is you lose your first three trades before winning your next seven. You have to plan for that. Work backwards — figure out your win-loss ratio first, then build your worst case scenario, then find the best risk management percentage that lets your account survive that scenario and still come out in profit.

q: How many pips are you willing to lose before closing a trade as a loss?
a: 30 pips is my number. If I enter a trade and I lose 30 pips, I chalk it up as a loss — maybe it was a bad entry on my part, maybe the market did something completely unexpected. But 30 pips is where I'm out. I wouldn't make it 40 or 50 pips, something high like that. Try to keep it 20 to 30 pips and find what works based on your entry skills and technical analysis skills.

q: What three things do you need to establish before picking your risk management percentage?
a: One — find your win-loss ratio. Two — make your worst case scenario using that ratio. Three — find the best risk management percentage that suits your style of trading. If you're a conservative trader, you can't use a risky number. You can't go in with a lot or two lots on a 10–15% risk management — you just can't. Build that foundation first, then scale.

q: You blew four accounts before you figured this out — what actually happened?
a: I had no idea what risk management was. I blew four $1,000 accounts, lost $4,000 in the market. My first experience I abused leverage on gold, made $3,000 by the time the New York session opened, got greedy, and within 24 hours had $0 left. My next $1,000 account I was trading one, two lots, one and a half lots — blew it. Third account, blew it. Fourth account, blew it. Only when I found my mentor did I understand that risk management is the absolute basis — everything revolves around it.

q: What killed your success early on and what should traders watch out for?
a: I got something called greedy. After I made $3,000 on that gold trade, my next 15 trades were all losing trades. By about my eighth losing trade I was like, you know what, I think I should put in a buy — actually, since I've been wrong seven, eight times, let me sell instead. Every trade was a losing trade and within 24 hours I had $0. Once you get greedy, that's what kills your success.

q: What is the biggest technical fuckup you see students making on their charts?
a: Precision. Lack of precision on everything — random lines, fibs, trendlines, zones. Every single thing I'm doing on the charts is always the same exact way. Fibs are drawn a certain way every single time. It's never drawn sloppy. When one person is doing it one way and another person is doing it another way, all of a sudden it's messy, it's annoying, it could be a lot smoother. Stay on the same track.

q: What's the most common mistake traders make when drawing trendlines?
a: Drawing them too steep. If you can't walk in it, it is invalid. If it's too steep, it's gonna be forced and it is gonna break — guarantee it's a waste. You want a nice angle, something like 19 to 31 degrees. Point A to point B, tip to tip, and the angle has to be something the market can actually maintain.

q: How should your random lines be drawn when pinpointing support or resistance?
a: You zoom in to where that area actually formed and you want to touch all the wicks of that entire area — that's the main thing. Not the highest point, not just one wick, but the level where you're hitting all the wicks. I like to bring it back a little bit to the outside so I get a little bit of the past data, then hold shift to keep it a straight line. Precision, precision, precision — it determines how consistent the methodology is.

q: Why do you draw zones without a border and with opacity settings the way you do?
a: Go to style, turn the border opacity all the way down to 0%, and only use background color. All you see is just color — nice contrast, nice flow. The zone starts at the close of the body and goes all the way down to the lowest point within that area. It looks clean, it doesn't clutter the chart. If your chart looks like it needs CPR, your analysis is gonna suffer with it.

q: What was the structure of the live trade you walked through in The Process video?
a: It was a sell order on a five-lot position based on a sell limit order at the red EMA on the M5 timeframe. I saw the weekly candle was bearish, the H4 and H1 both had broken structure, the daily showed a respected key level — all of it confirmed bearish continuation. The setup was inside a 30-35 pip consolidation range, so I wasn't trying to get 80 pips out of it. The trade took about three hours, closed at $1,132.74, roughly 21 pips at five lots.

q: How do you manage your attention across timeframes while you're actually in a live trade?
a: You're never staying on one timeframe the whole time. I'm going back and forth, back and forth — M5 to H1 to H4, confirming that all the moving averages are still being respected and nothing is breaking against me. Once the order gets hit, I start rotating to the other timeframes just to make sure everything is still correlated. It's not a one-screen situation — you're actively analyzing the entire time.

q: Why did you enter a sell limit instead of a market order on the live trade?
a: I caught it as a true lower high that the market was trying to eliminate but wasn't closing above. The red EMA on M5 was acting as a zone the market was trying to retest, so I put my sell limit right at that red EMA. The market hadn't completely crossed it and confirmed the downtrend on M15, so I knew it had more time to come back to that area — which it did. I had near zero drawdown because of that entry.

q: What does it mean to be in a consolidation zone and how does it affect your take profit target?
a: If the consolidation is only 30 pips, you're not trying to get 50 or 80 pips out of that order. That entire range was about 30-35 pips wide, so I knew exactly where the TP was going to be. You trade based on what the market is showing you — not what you want it to give you. Don't be greedy. Take what the market provides within that range.

q: What does 'never have urgency for the market to go in your favor' actually look like in practice?
a: In the live trade, I was waiting for what felt like a long time watching price slowly creep up toward my sell limit. The impatient person is going to move their limit closer — but you don't move your analysis. Once you put your sell limit in, let it stay there. If you analyze correctly it will go in your favor. If you start panicking and shifting things around, you're faking yourself out and closing prematurely — all type of bad shit follows.

q: How do you handle watching a five-lot order fluctuate on the screen without panicking?
a: Look at pips, not the money. On a five-lot order, the money moves very, very fast — it could be negative $1,000 in just 20 pips. If you're staring at the dollar amount the whole time, you're gonna scare yourself. 20 pips is not that much, but that dollar number is gonna look wild. You have to get your mental stability to a point where you're comfortable with how that lot size fluctuates — it took me time to get there from micro lots to standard lots.

q: What's the minimum risk-to-reward ratio you'll accept on a trade and why?
a: Minimum is 1 to 1 — and I hate doing that. When I was up 7.9 pips on the live trade and my stop loss was equally tight, that was a 1:1 situation and I called that out as the minimum you should be trading. I want 1 to 5, or 5 to 1, or a 10 to 1. Minimum risk, maximum reward — those bomb entries where you can maximize your gains without risking too much are what you're really looking for. Anything less than 1:1, you're gone.

q: Why do you place your stop loss after the trade is already open rather than immediately at entry?
a: Because you need the market to first find a level of resistance before you place your stops above the previous high point. That way your stop is based on the confirmed structure — the previous lower high in the short-term downtrend — not just a random pip count from entry. On the live trade, waiting until price found resistance made the stop so tight that the risk-to-reward ratio was, as I said, fucking ridiculous. About 5 to 1.

q: What's the difference between trading on a variable spread account versus a low-spread account like ECN when it comes to order activation?
a: On an ECN account like Eightcap, the spread is very, very tight — the market only has to pass your limit by about 0.6 pips to activate your order. On a variable spread account, you need closer to 1 to 1.4 pips of movement past the order level. That matters because on a tight account, a simple quick spike could trigger your entry in a perfect spot. On a variable spread account, that same spike might not fill you. Know what your broker requires.

q: What mistake are traders making when they see other people posting big wins with four or five lots on social media?
a: Don't get sidetracked and influenced by that. If you see someone trading four lots and only needing 20 pips to make thousands of dollars, that's because they have tens of thousands of dollars in their account to support that kind of position. The only thing getting influenced by that is going to do is make you make a bad decision. Not one single successful trader I know uses improper risk management and is successful today — not one.

q: When you notice price moving against you during a live trade, how do you decide whether to stay in or get out?
a: You analyze actively — you're not just watching the order, you're checking every timeframe to see if the structure is still being respected. On the live trade, when price pulled back from 7.9 pips down to 4 pips and then even lower, I kept looking at the H1 and saw the higher timeframe bearish engulfing still in play and the weekly still showing bearish momentum. As long as the analysis holds, you stay. Never close because the dollar number is moving fast.

q: What's the difference between noise on a lower timeframe chart and an actual directional move?
a: Noise is what's happening when the market is in a consolidation zone and just moving sideways in a tight range — most people can't read it, and that's fine. But once you're good enough at analysis, you should already know what's noise and what's a real move. On the live trade, all that small M5 fluctuation inside the 30-35 pip consolidation zone was pure noise — I knew it because the weekly and daily were still screaming bearish. The real move comes when momentum picks up after the noise clears.

q: What are the four main fears that destroy traders?
a: The four main fears are being wrong, losing money, missing out, and leaving money on the table. Every bad trade, every impulsive decision you've ever made came from one of those four. Being wrong makes people ignore what's right in front of them. Missing out makes people jump in before the candle even closes. You've gotta admit these fears exist before you can actually start trading smarter.

q: How does the fear of being wrong show up on the charts?
a: When you're in a buy and the market starts going against you, your brain locks onto every single bullish tick and blocks out everything else. It's like a pain reflex — your mind tries to self-preserve at any cost. You'll ignore the obvious because you already placed the order and you don't want to be wrong. If it's very obviously going the wrong way, just take the small L and come back with a stronger game plan.

q: Why do traders put their stop losses in the wrong place?
a: Most people set their stop loss based on how much money they're willing to lose — 'I only want to lose $20' — not based on structure. That's not a stop loss, that's just you being cheap. Your stop loss has to be in an area where it's least likely to actually get hit, outside of the fluctuation range. If you're not basing it on structure and price action, you're just placing a blind stop loss and wondering why you keep getting taken out.

q: What creates the fear of the market in the first place?
a: Fear of the market is just a lack of patience. You're entering in the middle of a move — between a higher low and a higher high — where price has easy room to fluctuate in either direction. That's not a base, that's the middle of nowhere. If you're rushing the process and not waiting for your higher low or lower high points, you're going to feel like the market is chasing you personally. The market isn't against you — you're just not in the right spot.

q: What happens to a trader's mindset after two or three losses in a row?
a: You start developing a scarcity mindset. Two losses and all of a sudden you want to withdraw the whole account because 'the market is against me.' That's just your lack of understanding of the asset and the setup requirements showing up as emotion. Get more educated on where your entries should actually be, where your stop loss ranges should be placed, and the fear starts dissolving. Confidence comes from comprehension, not from winning.

q: What's the real cause of greed in trading?
a: Greed is based on your appetite, and your appetite is based on where you are in life right now. If you're trading a 0.01 lot size, you're going to be in the markets all day chasing money because that profit amount will never change anything in your life. You're never satisfied so you keep going, buy sell buy sell, until you fall flat on your face. The fix is to save up and trade a lot size where catching your target number of pips actually satisfies you.

q: How should I set my first income target as a new trader?
a: Your first target should be to replace your current job income — that's it. If you're making $150 a day at your job, then $200 in one trading day is a great day. Don't try to go from zero to rich overnight. Once you consistently hit that replacement income level, then you start stacking on top of it. This is a long game and it took me a long time to understand that too.

q: How many good setups do I actually need in a week?
a: Throughout the entire week, all you really need is two or three good setups. Not ten, not twenty — two or three. When you're trying to catch every move, trading the main trend, the counter trend, and the consolidation all at once, you're going to find yourself flat on your face every time. The market is not something you can keep up with by brute-forcing every single candle. Quality, not quantity.

q: Why does comparing yourself to other traders make things worse?
a: You're always going to lose that comparison every single time. Somebody else's account size, lot size, and profit targets are based on their life and their satisfaction level — not yours. My numbers look different because I've been in this industry for ten years and my appetite has changed. Never compare because it creates an urgency that doesn't belong in your trading. Just focus on where you are and make progress from there.

q: What is the fear of missing out doing to your entries?
a: FOMO makes you take impulsive decisions. You see an explosive candle on the M30 or H1 and you jump in without even analyzing the chart. You didn't wait for the candle to close, you didn't check structure, nothing — you just jumped because you felt like you were going to miss it. And then it leaves a giant wick and you get wrecked. Let the candle close. That close is your final confirmation.

q: Why do traders close trades too early and leave money on the table?
a: Because they're scared that the last push is the last push before a reversal. But price action takes time, it fluctuates. You need to trust your analysis, trust your target projection, and watch price action in those target zones for clues. If you panic every time price ticks back a few pips before continuing, you're going to leave massive money on the table constantly. Small fluctuations are not reversals.

q: What does a tight stop loss tell you about a trader's psychology?
a: If you're putting a tight stop loss on every trade, you haven't actually accepted the risk. You want the market to only move in your favor immediately, which means you're using too much size and you're scared of losing money. When that stop gets taken out — and it will — you get emotional, you get vengeful, you get irrational. If tightening your stop makes you nervous, that's not a stop loss problem. That's a position sizing problem.

q: How does a trading plan help with the psychological side of trading?
a: A trading plan eliminates stupid mistakes. Selling at support, buying at resistance — that's a stupid mistake, and it happens when you have no plan to follow. Writing your plan out every single day soaks it into your subconscious. When you have a clear, clean plan, you stop looking for setups everywhere and start only seeing the quality ones that match your criteria. It gives you structure, and structure builds discipline.

q: What should I do immediately after a losing trade?
a: Study the loss first — take a screenshot, figure out what went wrong and what could have been better, and save it to a file. After that, step away from the charts. If you stay on the charts right after a loss, you're going to enter for the wrong reasons. That's revenge trading, and revenge trading will blow accounts fast. Walk away, clear your head, reset. Come back when you're actually in the right mental state.

q: Why is studying your losses more important than studying your wins?
a: If you study what you're losing on, you won't keep making those same mistakes. Think of it like a basketball player with a weak shooting arc — if you just keep shooting without fixing the mechanics, you'll keep missing. But once you identify the problem and fix it specifically, you become more consistent. Your wins will continue doing what they're doing. It's your losses that have the information you need to actually improve.

q: What does it mean when you say trading is a game of probabilities?
a: It means there is no guaranteed trade, ever. You can have four losses in a row before getting six consecutive wins. Being a 60% trader is all about probabilities — you don't know which trade is going to be the winner and which is going to be the loser. If you're trying to set things up so you never lose, you're already setting yourself up to fail. Accept the risk, manage the downside, and let probability work in your favor over time.

q: What's the danger of adding to a losing trade?
a: Once you add to a loser, you've got two positions going against you now. Then you add again trying to save it, and suddenly the whole account is on the line because of one trade you couldn't let go. I've done this — blew a $100 account in 20 minutes because I kept stacking on a gold trade that was going negative. Never add to a losing position. Only stack on winning trades, and only when there's a structural reason to do so.

q: How does never letting a winner turn into a loser protect you psychologically?
a: The frustration of watching 20 pips turn into a loss is one of the worst feelings in trading — it's demoralizing and it pushes you into desperate trades. Once you're up to a certain point, move your stop to at least breakeven. If your target is 30 pips, set a stop profit at 25 so even if it pulls back sharply you still walk away positive. You never want to feel like you played yourself, because that emotion creates bad habits fast.

q: Why does your mental state and environment matter before trading?
a: A messy space is a messy mind. You have to be awake and alert — not just technically awake, but showered, fed, shoes on, ready to work. I literally started putting on my shoes before trading sessions and it switched something in my brain to work mode. Your trading environment is a reflection of where your mind is. If you sit down chaotic and disorganized, your decisions will be chaotic and disorganized.

q: What's the difference between being awake and being alert when trading?
a: Being awake just means you're not asleep. Being alert means your brain is actually firing properly. You can wake up, grab your phone, and try to trade from bed while your mind is still half gone — that's awake, not alert. Alert means you've done the things that prime your brain: eaten right, moved around, gotten your body into work mode. Your trading time should match your peak mental performance window, whatever that is for your schedule.

q: How does fear of the market create a scarcity mindset?
a: When you get two losses back-to-back and you don't understand why, fear takes over and tells you to pull everything out and stop. That's scarcity — protecting yourself from a game you don't fully understand yet. The problem is that sitting out for weeks doesn't teach you anything. The real move is to get more educated on the asset, on where your entries should be, on why your stop placements are off. That education is what replaces fear with confidence.

q: Why does trading every single day work against you?
a: If you're in the market every single day, you're probably not satisfied with what your lot size is producing, so you're chasing. You're trying to be in every move — main trend, counter trend, consolidation — and that will leave you flat on your face every time. I try to stay out the market a lot because I'm working with a lot size where two or three good setups a week satisfies my appetite. Less is more when you're set up correctly.

q: What does greed look like in a small account trader?
a: Small account greed looks like trading 0.01 on every pair every single day because you need the money to move, but it never moves enough to feel like anything. So you keep going, keep trading, keep trying to catch every little move, and eventually you overtrade yourself into losses. The real answer isn't to trade more — it's to save up and build the account to a size where the lot you're comfortable with actually produces numbers that mean something to your life.

q: How do you know if you're trading with too much urgency?
a: If you're angry when a trade goes against you, if you get emotional, if you feel like you need to get your money back right now — that's urgency, and that's too much size. You should be able to watch a trade pull back and feel calm because you know your stop is placed correctly and your analysis is valid. I've said it before: never have urgency for the market to go in your favor. The moment you feel that urgency, check your position size.

q: What's the connection between lack of patience and the fear of the market?
a: If you're patient enough to wait for the base of a higher low on an uptrend or the base of a lower high on a downtrend, you get into entries where the market has to move significantly against the overall structure to stop you out. But people rush and enter in the middle of the range where price can easily fluctuate both directions and still maintain structure. That fluctuation feels like the market is targeting you personally. It's not — you just didn't wait for the right spot.

q: Where exactly do you put Point A and Point B when drawing a Fibonacci on a downtrend?
a: In a downtrend, Point A goes at the top of a bearish candle — the upper wick — that started the move down. Point B goes at the lower wick of the bearish candle where the market found support. It's bearish to bearish, and in an uptrend it's the opposite — bullish to bullish. Where you actually draw that Point A and Point B is everything, because it determines every single retracement level below it. Precision is the whole game.

q: What's the difference between the 23.6% and the 38.2% fib level?
a: Think of the 23.6 like a small speed bump — it barely slows the car down. The market can consolidate there but it's more of a continuation signal than a real reversal zone. The 38.2 is a legit PRZ, a price reversal zone. That's where you're seeing the market actually slow down and give you a real pullback opportunity. If the market only reaches 23.6 on a retrace, you gotta be careful because there's a ton of room left for it to keep pulling back before your entry gets invalidated.

q: Why is the 50% fib level not something you focus on?
a: At 50% the market is completely even — 50% buyers, 50% sellers. There's no edge there because you genuinely don't know what it's going to do. It's pure indecision, and I don't trade indecision. I want levels that tell me who's in control, and 50% tells me nothing. Skip it.

q: What does it mean when the market retraces all the way to the 88.6% fib level?
a: The 88.6 is the deepest PRZ — the maximum retracement before structure fully breaks. When you're seeing a pullback that deep, you're watching a trend within a trend unfold. The overall move is very slow because there are so many counter-pressure buyers or sellers fighting the main direction. Your stop room is the tightest at 88.6 compared to 23.6, which is actually good for risk management — less room to the 110% or 120% stop loss zone.

q: Where do you place your stop loss when trading off Fibonacci levels?
a: My preset stop loss areas are the 110% or 120% extension on the Fibonacci. Those levels sit just above the previous high or just below the previous low depending on direction. If the market hits 110% or 120%, structure is broken and you shouldn't be in that trade in the first place. The 110% is more conservative, the 120% gives you maximum breathing room. Either way, those two levels are your safety net — they exist so you're not riding a trade in the completely wrong direction.

q: What timeframe should I be drawing my Fibonacci on?
a: Draw your Fibonacci on the same timeframe you're basing your structure on — typically the H4 or Daily. If you're doing H4 structure, your fibs are H4 fibs. Don't get confused by lower timeframes showing a retrace to 38.2 on the H1 when your structure is H4 — that H1 touch is invalid for your trade because you're not basing structure there. Always stay in the same timeframe you started your analysis in.

q: How do you use the -27% and -61.8% fib extensions for take profit targets?
a: Once the market makes its move in your direction, the -27% is your first projection target for the continuation — that's a solid TP if you want to be technical about it. The -61.8% is further out if you want to push it. These are negative extensions, meaning below your Point B in a downtrend, giving you the projected distance the market can travel after the retracement plays out. If you want to maximize, you can also measure a new Point A to Point B off a fresh structure break and stack those targets.

q: How do you confirm that a 23.6% retrace is actually a valid sell entry?
a: You need to see consolidation in that zone first — the market should be hanging around 23.6, not just tapping it. Then you need a bearish variation candle closing below the prior support candle to confirm sellers are inside the market. If you don't have that bearish close, do not jump in — the market can easily keep pulling back to 38.2, 61.8, or higher. Confirmation is mandatory at 23.6 because that level has the most room to pull against you.

q: What is a break of structure and how do you confirm it on a specific timeframe?
a: A break of structure is when the market breaks a higher low point in an uptrend or a lower high point in a downtrend — whichever level was holding the trend. To confirm it on, say, H1, you need to see the market actually close through that support level on the H1 chart with a decisive candle — not just a wick. If you're doing H4 structure, it has to break on H4. An M30 candle closing through an H4 structure level doesn't count. Keep everything in the same timeframe.

q: After a break of structure, what's the first trade opportunity?
a: The first opportunity after a break of structure in a downtrend is the first lower high point — that's where you're looking to sell. In an uptrend reversal it's the first higher low point — that's your buy. That first retest after the break is your cleanest entry because it's the first new point confirming the direction change. Miss it and you're chasing.

q: Can a market still be in an uptrend even if it's making lower highs?
a: Yes — as long as the higher low points on the bottom are not being broken, the uptrend is still intact. The market can make equal highs, lower highs, it doesn't matter. What holds the trend is the higher lows on the bottom side. The moment a higher low gets broken, that's when you consider the structure broken. Same concept flipped for downtrends — lower highs don't define it, lower lows do.

q: How does Fibonacci connect to market structure?
a: They're inseparable. When you're measuring a retrace, the 100% Fibonacci level is literally that higher low point on your structure. If the market breaks that 100%, structure is broken — it's not just a Fibonacci thing, it's a structural break at the same time. This is why I say Fibonacci is studied right after structure, because every retrace you measure on your structure is a fib, and every fib zone you watch is tied to a structural level. You're using them together on every single trade.

q: What are the three situations you look for when drawing support and resistance levels?
a: A touch, a gap, and an overlap. A touch is the most precise — the line graph price closes directly on the level both times. A gap is when there's a small space between the two price points hitting the level. An overlap is when the price points slightly cross each other. For the gap and overlap, you split the middle so the line sits evenly between both sides. The touch is king for precision.

q: Why do you use a line chart instead of candlesticks when drawing support and resistance?
a: The line chart only shows closed prices, which filters out all the noise — the wicks, the spikes, the manipulation. That's how you find where price truly respected a level versus where it just briefly pierced through. Once you draw your levels off the line chart, you go back to candlesticks and you'll see those zones got tested with indecision candles, consolidation, wicks all over the place. It makes your levels a lot more precise.

q: How many support and resistance levels should you draw on the H4 timeframe?
a: One, two, maximum three. That's it. Don't go drawing ten lines on your chart — that's just noise and it'll confuse your analysis. You're looking for the most significant areas of indecision at H4, the ones where the market clearly paused and reacted. Keep it clean, keep it simple. Daily is the same concept but even more selective.

q: For how long does a support or resistance level remain valid?
a: If you draw them correctly on the Daily and H4 timeframes, they can stay valid for two to three weeks or even longer. That's the advantage of working off higher timeframes — those levels have more weight behind them and the market keeps coming back to respect them. This is why I don't bother drawing S&R on H1 anymore. H1 is too noisy and the levels break down faster.

q: What is a 'random line' and how do you use it in your analysis?
a: A random line is just a blue line I drop on the chart in real time when I'm inside a move and I notice the market is reacting to a level I haven't formally marked yet. It's not a pre-drawn support or resistance level — it's something I spot on the fly. Could be a quick area of support, a resistance zone, or even a trendline. It's flexible and has no rigid rules. If the market is showing me something, I use the random line to mark it so I don't lose track of it.

q: How do moving averages tell you whether you're in a trending or consolidating market?
a: When the MAs are below price, you're in a bullish trend — the market has upward momentum. When the MAs are above price, you're in a bearish trend with selling pressure. When the MAs are in between the price action, cutting through the candles, that's consolidation — and I stay out of those markets unless I can zoom up to a higher timeframe to get clarity. Clean and simple: below is bullish, above is bearish, in between is choppy.

q: What two moving averages do you use and what are their settings?
a: I use two simple moving averages — not exponential, simple. The first is the 66-period SMA in black, second thickness. The second is the 85-period SMA in green, second thickness. Both are set to close with everything else at default. I've been running these same two for four or five years now and they've served me well across every asset class.

q: Why is it important to match your entry timeframe to your structure timeframe when using fibs?
a: Because if your structure is H4 and you're watching an H1 chart, the H1 might show a retrace that looks like it's at 38.2 — but based on the H4, the market barely moved. The H4 is what matters if that's where you drew your fib. You can drop to a lower timeframe to get a more precise entry, but you never lose sight of the bigger timeframe you're trading from. That's how you stay four to five pips better on your entry versus just throwing an order at a round number.

q: What's the significance of a descending triangle pattern within market structure?
a: A descending triangle in an uptrend shows the market making lower highs while holding the same support level on the bottom. As long as that support on the bottom is not broken, the uptrend is still valid. The lower highs on top are just the market compressing before its next move. The same concept works inverted in a downtrend. Trendlines help you map that compression in real time so you're not caught off guard when it finally breaks.

q: How do you use fibs to confirm you're at a valid higher low point?
a: A higher low in structure connects directly to the 100% level on your Fibonacci. As long as the market hasn't broken that 100% mark — which is your previous low — you're still in a higher low formation and the structure is intact. The deeper retrace levels like 78.6 and 88.6 are still valid higher low entries as long as that 100% is not breached. Once it breaks 100%, the higher low is gone and you need to reassess the entire trend.

q: When you see a bearish engulfing candle at the 38.2% fib on the H4, what are you doing next?
a: That's the signal I'm waiting for. A bearish engulfing at the 38.2 on H4 tells me sellers are inside the market at the first real PRZ of this retracement. I'm now dropping to H1 or M30 to find a structure-based entry — looking for a lower high to form on that timeframe confirming continuation down. The H4 setup gives me the direction and the confluence, the lower timeframe gives me the precision. That combo is where the best setups live.

q: What does it mean when a support or resistance level acts on both sides of price?
a: That's what makes it a valid level — it has to act as both support and resistance at different points in time. If I see a level that only got hit from one side, I'm not drawing it. It needs to have acted as a floor at some point and then acted as a ceiling at another point, or vice versa. That dual role tells me the market has serious memory at that price zone and it's worth putting on the chart.

q: How do you know when to drop to a lower timeframe after identifying your fib zone on H4?
a: Once the market reaches your key Fibonacci level on H4 — say 38.2% — you drop to H1 or M30 to watch how price is actually behaving in that zone. You're looking for a structure-based entry: a lower high forming, a bearish variation candle closing below a prior candle. The H4 told you where to look, the lower timeframe tells you exactly when to get in. That top-down approach is what gives you precision — the H4 does 30% of the work, the M5 or M30 does the other 60%.

q: What's the real difference between a demo account and a live account — aren't they basically the same market?
a: The market itself is identical — if price spikes up on my live account, it spikes up on your demo account too. The difference is slippage, spread, and psychology. On demo, you don't experience slippage because you're not actually going to market to fill that position. But the bigger thing is this: you can lose a hundred thousand dollars on demo in one night and still sleep just fine — real money, you're not forgetting that trade.

q: How do I use demo the right way so it actually prepares me for live trading?
a: Practice on demo exactly how you'd trade live — same balance, same lot size, same methodology, same order of operations. Don't be over there trading 10 lots on demo when you only have enough to do a 0.5 on your live account. That's building false confidence. You're seeing big money move on demo but you haven't built the mental callous to handle that fluctuation when it's real.

q: Why do some traders perform well on demo but blow up on live?
a: On demo you don't really care — you can lose 80k, a hundred thousand in one night and not care, still sleep fine. Real money, that's bills, that's vacation, that's comfort. The psychological pressure is completely different when it's real. That money fluctuating up, down, positive, negative, positive, negative — that is mentally draining, and a lot of people can't handle it.

q: What is slippage and why should I care about it on live but not demo?
a: Slippage is when you place a large position and the market can't fill the entire lot at one price, so it fills you at a worse price to complete the order. On demo it fills whatever amount instantly because it's not actually going to market. I've done five blocks on US30 and been immediately down $25,000 before the trade even moved — after closing at what looked like a hundred thousand profit, only 75k hit my account because of initial slippage.

q: How long should I stay on demo before going live?
a: Don't be on demo for too long — get in the game. NBA players still practice, but you have to start playing the game at some point. You can't practice all day and never apply it. Every now and again get onto a live account and practice those same things, because that's what builds your mental over time. It builds a callous so you're not feeling drained by anxiety every time you're in the live market.

q: What's the ADX indicator and why should I use it?
a: ADX stands for the Average Directional Movement Index — it's a trend-based indicator that tells you when the market is in a trending type of movement versus a non-trending one. You only want to be trading in a trending market, mainly because setups are much cleaner and you can make a lot more from them. When the black ADX line is below 20, that market is non-trending — you shouldn't even really be trading it much.

q: What are the three lines on the ADX and what does each one mean?
a: The black line is the main ADX line — that's the primary one you watch. The green line is the positive D line, and the red line is the negative D line. When the green is above 25 and 20, you're in a bullish trending market. When the red is above 25 and 20, you're in a bearish trending market.
