When both the positive and negative are splitting further apart, there's no doubt the trend is going to continue for a while.

q: What ADX levels do I need to see before I consider taking a trade?
a: You need the ADX black line above 20 to be in a trending market, and above 25 means it's a valid trend — no doubt about it. When it's between 20 and 25 with the positive and negative lines consolidated in the middle, that's a consolidating market and you don't want to touch it. I personally set horizontal lines at both 20 and 25 so I can see exactly where I stand at a glance.

q: How do I use the ADX to hold a trade instead of exiting too early?
a: If you're in a sell and the negative D line is still above 25, you hold the order. The market is still showing a lot of bearish momentum. Some people see a pullback and exit, but looking at the ADX and seeing that red line staying above 25 the whole time — that's your signal to stay in. That's the kind of thing that takes a trade from decent to dramatic.

q: What timeframes does Cue focus on when using the ADX?
a: The main chart I use it on is the one-hour and the four-hour, but primarily the one-hour — that's what I focus on. Normally in the London session, the ADX line breaks above 25 and 20, so if you're seeing that type of movement in London, start preparing your entry based on your technical analysis and what the ADX is saying. Combine this with your confluence knowledge and you're going to be beyond profitable.

q: What is the Ichimoku cloud and what's the quick benefit of it?
a: The Ichimoku Kinko Hyo — I just call it the Ichimoku cloud — is what's known as a one-glance indicator. In seconds, just by looking at where price is relative to the cloud, I can determine if the trend is tradeable or if I should wait for a better setup. It shows me bias direction, trend strength, and potential support and resistance areas all at the same time.

q: What are the three main lines of the Ichimoku and what settings does Cue use?
a: The red line is the conversion line — what I call the fast SMA — and I set it to a period of 8. The blue line is the baseline, also called the standard reference line, set to a period of 21. The green line is the lagging line, set to 52 with an offset of 26. Those are my exact settings — use those.

q: What two confirmations do I need from the Ichimoku before placing a trade?
a: First, price needs to be outside the cloud — either fully above it or fully below it. Second, the red conversion line and the blue baseline need to cross. You need both. If price is closed below the cloud but the red and blue lines haven't crossed yet, you don't have your second confirmation — wait. I've seen people get excited and jump in early, only for price to reverse right back into the cloud.

q: How does the thickness of the Ichimoku cloud change how I read support and resistance?
a: When the cloud is thick, the support or resistance is strong — meaning if price is in a bearish move and approaches a thick cloud, it could reverse before even breaking through. When the cloud is thin, that support and resistance is weak and price can break through it more easily. Always check the thickness before assuming price will push through.

q: How do I tell if the market has a bullish or bearish bias using just the Ichimoku?
a: Watch whether price is closing above or below the blue baseline. Price closing consistently above the blue line means you're in a bullish bias. Price closing consistently below it means you're in a bearish bias. It's that simple — don't overcomplicate the cloud. It's designed to be read in one glance.

q: What are pivot points and why do you use them?
a: Pivot points are weekly calculated levels that traders use to determine quickly where market sentiment may shift between bullish and bearish. I personally love them for plotting horizontal support and resistance levels without doing a ton of manual work. The pivot line itself is the area where price can flip from bullish to bearish and vice versa — it's a simple, clean addition to any analysis.

q: Which pivot levels are the strongest and what typically happens there?
a: S1 and R1 — the first level of support and the first level of resistance — those are the strongest. That's where you're most likely to see a reversal. As price breaks through S1 or R1, each subsequent level gets weaker and weaker. I've seen price run from the first support level about 200 pips all the way back to the pivot in one move — those levels are real.

q: How do I combine pivot points with other tools in my analysis?
a: Pivot points are meant to add another layer of confirmation — that's it. I love pairing them with trendlines. I found a perfect trendline on gold on a four-hour chart where price had touched it three times, and the reversal happened right at a pivot point. If your other indicators say buy but price is sitting at R1 on the pivot, slow down — that resistance is real and could kill the trade.

q: How many weeks of pivot point data should I have on my chart?
a: I change the default from 15 weeks down to 5. You don't need to see 15 weeks worth of pivots cluttering your chart. Keep it clean — five is enough to give you context on recent levels without making the chart look like a mess. Clarity in your chart setup is part of having a clear process.

q: What are the three types of channels I should be looking for in the market?
a: The three types are an ascending channel — which goes up, a descending channel — which goes down, and a lateral channel — which is price just ranging sideways. For ascending and descending channels you use the parallel channel tool on TradingView. For lateral channels there's no dedicated tool, so you just use two horizontal lines. All three can give you breakout opportunities.

q: How many points does it take to confirm a channel or trendline?
a: Two points create the trendline, three points confirm it. Once that third touch happens, the trendline is confirmed and you can start trading off it as support or resistance. The more times price touches a trendline, the stronger it becomes — that's been true every single time I've used this approach.

q: How do you use channels to catch a breakout trade?
a: In an ascending channel, the top acts as resistance and the bottom acts as support. When price approaches the top of a confirmed channel and reverses, that's your play. The breakout happens when price fails to bounce off support and breaks below the channel floor — that's your sell setup. I've caught breakouts on silver going from the top of a channel all the way down in a very clean move, and I use pivot points to target how far I'll hold the order.

q: How do I set my profit target when trading a channel breakout?
a: Once you catch the breakout, I use pivot points to target how long I hold the order open. Whatever the nearest pivot point level is in the direction of the breakout — that's my initial target. If it's a bearish breakout, I'm looking at the first support level or the pivot itself. The pivot point tells you where price sentiment may shift, so that's a logical place to consider taking profit.

q: Why is a lateral channel breakout worth paying attention to even though price is just ranging?
a: A lateral channel is just price ranging — but eventually that range breaks one way or the other. When it does, the breakout can be significant. I've seen price sitting in a lateral range and then break out for almost 300 pips. The longer price consolidates in a tight range, the more energy it's building up for that move. That's the kind of breakout you want to be positioned for.

q: How did you actually build consistency in your trading?
a: Consistency happens by staying in the game — realizing what I'm doing wrong, taking a step back, reanalyzing, not being in such a rush to be in the market and be a part of every fluctuation. Sitting on my hands more, trying to find better entry areas so I see less drawdown. All those things I had to fix. It doesn't just happen, you earn it.

q: What happened when you blew multiple accounts back to back?
a: I blew a thousand dollar account and knew my debit card number by heart — CVC, expiration, everything — so I just deposited again immediately. Blown. Deposited again. Blown. Within about an hour and a half I lost around $3,500 to $4,000. My bank was calling asking if it was fraud and I'm telling them run it, run it. That was all-time high revenge trading right there.

q: What's the biggest mistake people make after blowing an account?
a: Jumping right back into trading immediately. You gotta take that time off and really understand what went wrong versus feeling like, yo, that account's blown, bet, next account immediately. The market can chew up 10,000, 100,000, 200,000 — there's no limit. Just because you have money does not mean you can make money.

q: Why do you think fundamentals threw you off during certain weeks?
a: There's been weeks where technically everything was a clean, smooth setup — smooth this, smooth that — but I was looking too deep into the news and it threw me off completely. I took unnecessary losses I could have avoided. Genuinely, f**k fundamentals. My technicals have never let me down, and every time I stray from them I end up giving money back for no reason.

q: If you mess up one week, what should you do?
a: It's all good. You're still good. Step back, reanalyze, and keep on going. Money could be lost, money could be made — keep that in mind. Don't let one bad week convince you to change your whole approach or make irrational decisions trying to recover. Dust off, reanalyze, and show back up.

q: Why is breaking even on a trade actually a problem, not a solution?
a: Being right and being early means you are wrong — that's rule number six in the Wall Street Academy notebook. If you're even thinking about moving your stop loss to break even, technically your analysis was shaky from the start. I've seen people set break even, get stopped out, and then watch that thing take off without them over and over again. You're not protecting yourself, you're just waving the white flag early.

q: How does journaling actually help you improve?
a: I've been journaling way more and I started noticing patterns in my mistakes — getting out early on the lower timeframe, moving stop losses to break even, and killing my own big wins. That data shows you exactly where you're losing money before you even realize it. Without that record you're just guessing at what's wrong.

q: What mental state do you need before you even open the charts?
a: If you are not stable mentally, start the day off before you get on the charts by taking a couple minutes to just breathe. Big deep breaths in, big deep breaths out for about a couple minutes. You're taking on voluntary stress every single day you trade — you gotta prep yourself for that. Coming in chaotic means you're already at a disadvantage.

q: What's the real cost of trading without a stop loss?
a: I used to say f**k stop losses too, until I got those trades where I lost a hundred thousand, $200,000 — and none of it ever had to be that much. The ego that says I don't need a stop loss will kill your pockets. It's always better to lose a trade than to lose an entire account based on one trade.

q: How do you know when you're ready to up your lot size?
a: You gotta ask yourself: can my mental handle this kind of fluctuation? The profit is sweet, but what's the opposite side of profit? Drawdown. Getting used to that part mentally is not easy. Never be in a rush to jump and up your lot size — scale into it versus trying to slam one full lot size on. Those negatives come a lot faster than you'd expect and your whole analysis goes out the window.

q: What does it actually take to stick with trading long enough to get consistent?
a: Not everybody wants to go through a hundred-plus hours of content, commit to the headache, commit to the 'I don't get it' moments, crying at night. This s**t is not for the weak. Anybody that's stuck with this thing — real rap — I gotta respect it because most people hear what it takes and walk right away.

q: What made you feel confident enough to leave your job and trade full time?
a: Even when I was at Target I was having thousand dollar days, and I was showing my coworkers like, yo, listen, this is what I do outside of work. My checks clearly weren't paying for my 2015 335 BMW and they could tell. It wasn't life-changing money yet, but I understood the potential of it. Once you understand the potential, you understand this s**t is worth committing to fully.

q: How do you handle it when the market goes 90% towards your stop loss and you're second-guessing yourself?
a: There are times that the market goes 98% towards your stop loss and never hits, then turns around completely. You have to commit to your risk. Once you go in the trade you have to start committing — just because you don't wanna lose money will cause you to lose more money. That's the difference between someone who's consistent and someone who's always playing games with their own positions.

q: Why do you keep showing up every week to do the QCast and Chart N Chill even when the market is slow?
a: We still get in work regardless of the fact. Even going to a birthday party, I'm still prepping you for the week. You have to maintain your discipline of showing up and analyzing because the market is always moving. If you only analyze when it's exciting, you'll miss the setups that actually build your account.

q: What's the danger of feeling like having money means you can make money in the market?
a: That's probably the scariest thing a person can experience in trading. As long as you're throwing money at the market, it can take everything — 10,000, a hundred thousand, 200,000, 300,000. There's no limit. You still gotta put in study time, put in the effort to understand what you're doing. Money without knowledge is just a donation.

q: How does having a smaller stop loss actually make your loss rate worse?
a: If you wanna risk less, dumb down your lot size. Don't keep the same lot size with a smaller stop loss — that s**t is gonna get hit every single time, guaranteed. If you put your stop loss at 88.6% on the fib, you're still inside the fluctuation range. That area could get tested and the trade is still perfectly valid for the original direction.

q: How do you develop your edge over time as a trader?
a: For me it was just recognizing patterns very, very well — that came easier over time. Developing edge was more just understanding what I'm doing, understanding what I'm doing wrong, understanding what I have to do in different situations. My edge now is being able to maximize a trade to the best of its ability. That takes practice and just not being scared to risk, but also being confident enough to know what you're doing when the chart is moving.

q: What does it look like when someone is taking their stress out on the charts?
a: I went through a rough breakup and I gravitated to the charts for comfort knowing I was going through something. But in reality it made me go a little more crazy because I was losing money I didn't have to lose — rent coming up, insurance coming up, and I'm just f**king up. The market is never gonna comfort you. It's not with you, it's trying to take your money. Your mental state always shows in your trading.

q: What's the benefit of prop firm challenges for building patience and discipline?
a: A challenge account is good for the person that has patience and discipline — the parameters don't allow you to go crazy, which means you're being forced to be patient. And at the other side of the coin, you're not investing thousands of dollars of your own money to make great money when you pass. It mentally preps you in ways that a live account doesn't always give you the structure to learn.

q: Why do you say the market can always be analyzed no matter what the environment looks like?
a: If a person says they can't analyze a chart because it's too sloppy, they probably don't have enough chart time. The markets can always be analyzed — it might take a bit more time, it might take a different timeframe, it might just take time to really understand what the f**k is going on. I've seen people say the market is unreadable and then I'm making $80,000 in a day. Your skill level determines what you see.

q: How do you approach a week where you messed up your analysis completely?
a: I messed up because I was looking too much into fundamentals and it threw off my read on a textbook 38.2% entry at the MAs. I had to own it — I should have looked at it more technically. But you don't carry that into the next week. You analyze it, understand what went wrong, and get better. I wasn't consistent enough back then, but staying in the game and correcting those mistakes is how the consistency eventually developed.

q: What's wrong with trying to approach the market the same exact way every single week?
a: It's like saying what two numbers give you seven — three and four, five and two, six and one, seven and zero. There's so many ways the market can give you the same direction. To approach it the same way every single week won't progress you as much as you want because it's never gonna look the same. You have to be open to adapting to new market environments — that's the most important skill.

q: How do you handle losing streak psychology without revenge trading?
a: Being comfortable taking the loss and not feeling like you have to make it up immediately — that's the whole thing. What I was doing when I kept reloading that account was clearly revenge trading, trying to make it back within the next couple minutes. The most you can do is dumb down your lot size and protect your mental. Multiple losses back to back is draining, but a tighter stop loss does not help that — it makes it worse.

q: What's the role of a trade-ready checklist in staying consistent?
a: I dropped a trade-ready document and a no-trade document — things to look for and things to avoid. If a few of these things line up, take the trade. If a few of the no-trade conditions are present, don't. Print it out, put it on your whiteboard, keep it next to your desk. Even I need to see these things in front of me to make sure I'm not missing steps in my analysis. Structure and discipline over everything.
